items.title: Stroud and Gloucester Road Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act from 1818 authorizing the construction, maintenance, and likely toll collection for a turnpike road between Stroud and Gloucester.
items.reason: This 200-year-old local act is obsolete; the road is now part of the public highway system governed by modern legislation. Retaining it clutters the statute books, imposes unseen costs via legal uncertainty (e.g., lawyers parsing archaic conflicts), distorts incentives by preserving outdated property/toll claims, and hinders institutional clarity without delivering any current benefits to prosperity or liberty.

items.title: Turnpike Roads in Peebles Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act from 1818 authorizing the establishment, construction, maintenance, and toll collection for turnpike roads in Peebles, Scotland, by trustees or a turnpike trust.
items.reason: This 19th-century act is a dead letter, as all UK turnpike trusts were dissolved by the late 1800s under subsequent legislation (e.g., Turnpike Acts Continuance Acts and the Highways Act 1835 onwards), with roads integrated into the public highway system maintained by local authorities without tolls. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: statutory clutter confuses legal research, risks frivolous litigation claiming archaic toll or property rights, distorts incentives by preserving obsolete institutional relics that hinder clear modern property titles, and exemplifies regulatory creep where historical laws linger without delivering value, preventing a lean legal framework conducive to liberty and prosperity.

items.title: Road from Newcastle-upon-Tyne to the Alemouth Turnpike Road Act 1818
items.summary: An 1818 local Act (c. xi) authorizing the establishment of a turnpike trust for the construction, maintenance, and toll collection on the road from Newcastle-upon-Tyne to Alnmouth.
items.reason: This 200-year-old local Act is obsolete, as turnpike trusts were largely dissolved by the late 19th century, with roads now publicly maintained by highways authorities under modern legislation. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: cluttering the statute book, potential legal uncertainty if referenced in disputes over historical rights-of-way or property, and administrative burden on government resources for archiving and reviewing defunct laws, distorting focus from current priorities without providing any ongoing benefit to liberty, property, or prosperity.

items.title: Manchester and Wilmslow Road Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act 1818 (c. xii) concerning the establishment, maintenance, and likely toll operations for the Manchester and Wilmslow Road, a historical turnpike road.
items.reason: This 200-year-old local act is obsolete, as the road is now integrated into the modern public highway system under current national legislation. Keeping it on the books creates unseen costs: legal redundancy, potential conflicts with contemporary road management laws, administrative bloat, and reduced clarity in the statute book, which distorts incentives for efficient infrastructure and burdens property rights without delivering benefits.

items.title: Towcester and Cotton End Road Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act of 1818 authorizing the construction, maintenance, and likely tolling of a turnpike road between Towcester and Cotton End in Northamptonshire.
items.reason: This 1818 local act is long obsolete, superseded by modern highways legislation and national infrastructure management. Keeping it on the books creates unseen costs including legal uncertainty, potential jurisdictional overlaps, administrative burdens in statute review and archival maintenance, and distortion of clear property rights over roads, without delivering any current benefits to liberty or prosperity.

items.title: Hagley and Birmingham Road Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act authorizing the establishment, maintenance, or tolling of the road between Hagley and Birmingham (likely a turnpike road improvement scheme).
items.reason: This 1818 local act is obsolete, as 19th-century turnpike and road acts have been superseded by modern highways legislation (e.g., Highways Act 1980). Keeping it creates unseen costs: legal clutter increasing compliance burdens for landowners and developers, potential for archaic clauses to conflict with current property rights and infrastructure projects, and administrative waste in maintaining irrelevant historical statutes. Deletion simplifies the legal system, reduces uncertainty, and frees resources without harming road maintenance, which occurs via contemporary institutions.

items.title: Whitehaven Harbour and Improvement Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act (1818 c. xv) authorizing improvements to Whitehaven Harbour and associated town infrastructure, likely involving construction, tolls, and governance powers for the harbor.
items.reason: This 200+ year-old local act is almost certainly obsolete, superseded by modern ports legislation, privatization, or repeal, rendering it inert. Keeping it on the books creates unseen costs: legal clutter complicating research and interpretation, potential for archaic clauses to conflict with current property rights and trade laws, administrative burdens on archives and courts, and distortion of incentives by preserving outdated public authority structures that hinder private harbor management and innovation.

items.title: Thames and Medway Canal Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act authorizing the construction, operation, and maintenance of the Thames and Medway Canal, including powers for land acquisition, tolls, and management by a canal company.
items.reason: This 1818 infrastructure act is obsolete; the canal was largely abandoned by the early 20th century due to railways and roads, with any remnants superseded by modern transport laws or derelict. Retaining it clutters the legal system, raises compliance and research costs for property transactions, and risks dormant rights distorting current land use or development incentives, preventing efficient allocation of resources to higher-value uses without achieving any ongoing public benefit.

items.title: Birmingham Canal Navigations Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act authorizing the construction, maintenance, and regulation of canal navigations in Birmingham, including powers for land acquisition, tolls, and operations.
items.reason: This 1818 local act is obsolete, as canal operations are now managed by the Canal & River Trust under modern statutes like the Transport Act 1968 and Waterways Act 1971. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: legal complexity for referencing archaic provisions in disputes, potential toll or usage restrictions distorting current leisure/commercial use, higher compliance burdens on private operators, and clogging statute books which hinders efficient property rights and innovation in waterways management. No evidence it uniquely achieves navigation benefits today that markets couldn't provide via contract or updated law.

items.title: York and Boroughbridge Road Act 1818
items.summary: York and Boroughbridge Road Act 1818: A local act authorising the establishment, maintenance, and likely toll collection for a turnpike road connecting York and Boroughbridge in England.
items.reason: This 200-year-old infrastructure act is obsolete, superseded by modern highways legislation (e.g., Highways Act 1980). Retaining it clogs the statute book, imposes archival and research costs on citizens and businesses, risks unintended legal conflicts in property or planning disputes, distorts incentives by obscuring clear current law (contra Hayek's emphasis on predictable rules), and yields zero ongoing benefits as the road is now a public highway without turnpike operations.

items.title: Leicester County Offices and Judges' Accommodations Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act authorizing the provision of county offices and accommodations for judges in Leicester, enacted in 1818.
items.reason: This 200-year-old local infrastructure act has long fulfilled any original purpose, with buildings likely superseded or irrelevant today. Keeping it imposes unseen costs like cluttering the statute book, complicating legal research, and perpetuating obsolete government interventions that distort focus from modern priorities; deletion eliminates regulatory deadweight without harming liberty or property rights, as no reliance exists.

items.title: Folkestone Harbour Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act authorizing the construction, management, and operation of Folkestone Harbour, including likely powers for tolls, works, and governance specific to the site.
items.reason: This 200-year-old local act grants bespoke powers and potential monopolistic privileges for a single harbour, now superseded by modern infrastructure laws, planning regulations, and private management. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: legal clutter hindering property transfers or updates, distorted incentives via outdated tolls or restrictions, and barriers to innovation as operators navigate archaic rules instead of market-driven decisions. Deletion removes these distortions, allowing full private property rights and contractual freedom to enhance prosperity without government relics.

items.title: Chipping Barnet Churchyard Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act from 1818 regulating the churchyard in Chipping Barnet, likely concerning maintenance, burials, or enlargement, typical of 19th-century parish-specific measures to address local burial space shortages.
items.reason: This 206-year-old hyper-local act addresses a transient 19th-century issue of churchyard overcrowding, now superseded by modern general laws on church property (e.g., Church of England faculties, Local Government Acts) and planning regulations. Retaining it clutters the statute book, risks unintended conflicts with current property rights or development (e.g., blocking repurposing of disused graveyards), distorts incentives for land use, and imposes compliance costs on parishes or locals without measurable benefits. Deletion simplifies the legal landscape, enhances property liberty, and avoids unseen distortions from obsolete rules, with no evidence of ongoing necessity.

items.title: Cirencester and Stroud, and Cirencester and Bisley Roads Act 1818
items.summary: Local Act from 1818 authorizing the construction, maintenance, and likely tolling of roads connecting Cirencester, Stroud, and Bisley in Gloucestershire, establishing a turnpike trust.
items.reason: This 206-year-old local infrastructure act is obsolete; modern roads are managed by public highways authorities with no reliance on 19th-century turnpike monopolies. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: clutters the statute book, creates legal uncertainty for property owners and developers who must research defunct rights/obligations, distorts incentives by preserving archaic toll or maintenance claims, and diverts parliamentary resources from relevant deregulation. Deletion imposes zero costs as no modern outcomes depend on it.
